content.text: India is a vast country with extremely varied landforms. Geologically, the country displays great diversity with different types of terrain representing different ages of formation. The Peninsular Plateau is one of the ancient landmasses, while the Himalayas and Northern Plains represent the most recent landforms. India possesses practically all major physical features of the earth including mountains, plains, deserts, plateaus, and islands.

From a geological perspective, the Himalayan mountains form an unstable zone with youthful topography characterised by high peaks, deep valleys, and fast-flowing rivers. The northern plains, formed of alluvial deposits, are among the most recent landforms. The Peninsular Plateau, composed of igneous and metamorphic rocks with gently rising hills and wide valleys, represents ancient and relatively stable landmass.

The physical features of India can be grouped under six major physiographic divisions: (1) The Himalayan Mountains, (2) The Northern Plains, (3) The Peninsular Plateau, (4) The Indian Desert, (5) The Coastal Plains, and (6) The Islands. These divisions reflect different geological origins, topographical characteristics, and environmental conditions.

The Himalayas are geologically young fold mountains that stretch over the northern borders of India. These mountain ranges run in a west-east direction from the Indus to the Brahmaputra, covering a distance of about 2,400 km. Their width varies from 400 km in Kashmir to 150 km in Arunachal Pradesh. The altitudinal variations are greater in the eastern half than in the western half.

The Himalayas consist of three parallel ranges in their longitudinal extent, with a number of valleys lying between these ranges. These three ranges are the Great Himalayas (Himadri), the Lesser Himalayas (Himachal), and the Outer Himalayas (Shiwaliks).

The northernmost range is known as the Great Himalayas or Himadri. It is the most continuous range consisting of the loftiest peaks with an average height of 6,000 metres. This range contains all the prominent Himalayan peaks including Mount Everest (8,848 m), Kanchenjunga (8,598 m), Makalu (8,481 m), Dhaulagiri (8,172 m), and Nanda Devi (7,817 m).

The folds of the Great Himalayas are asymmetrical in nature. The core of this part consists of granite. It is perennially snow-bound, and numerous glaciers descend from this range. The Great Himalayas form the most formidable mountain barrier, with deep erosional valleys and river gorges.

The range lying south of the Himadri forms the most rugged mountain system and is known as the Himachal or Lesser Himalaya. The ranges are mainly composed of highly compressed and altered rocks. The altitude varies between 3,700 and 4,500 metres with an average width of 50 km.

Important ranges within the Himachal include the Pir Panjal range (the longest and most important), the Dhaula Dhar, and the Mahabharat ranges. This region is famous for its beautiful valleys such as the valley of Kashmir, the Kangra and Kullu valleys in Himachal Pradesh. The region is well-known for its scenic hill stations like Mussoorie, Nainital, and Ranikhet.

The outermost range of the Himalayas is called the Shiwaliks. They extend over a width of 10-50 km with an altitude varying between 900 and 1,100 metres. These ranges are composed of unconsolidated sediments brought down by rivers from the main Himalayan ranges located farther north.

The longitudinal valleys lying between the Lesser Himalayas and the Shiwaliks are known as Duns. Dehra Dun, Kotli Dun, and Patli Dun are some well-known examples. These dun valleys are covered with thick gravel and alluvium deposits.

Besides the longitudinal divisions, the Himalayas have been divided on the basis of regions from west to east. The part between Indus and Satluj is traditionally known as Punjab Himalaya (also called Kashmir and Himachal Himalaya). The part between Satluj and Kali rivers is known as Kumaon Himalayas. The Nepal Himalayas lie between Kali and Teesta rivers. The part between Teesta and Dihang rivers is known as Assam Himalayas.

Beyond the Dihang gorge, the Himalayas bend sharply southward. These eastern extensions are known as the Purvachal or Eastern Hills and Mountains. These hills running through the northeastern states consist of the Patkai hills, Naga hills, Manipur hills, and Mizo hills, mostly composed of strong sandstones and sedimentary rocks.

The Northern Plain has been formed by the interplay of three major river systems: the Indus, the Ganga, and the Brahmaputra, along with their tributaries. This plain is formed of alluvial soil, representing one of the most recent landforms. The deposition of alluvium over millions of years at the foothills of the Himalaya has formed this fertile plain.

The plain spreads over an area of 7 lakh square kilometres and is approximately 2,400 km long and 240 to 320 km broad. It is a densely populated physiographic division. With rich soil cover, adequate water supply from rivers, and favourable climate, it is agriculturally a productive part of India.

In the lower course of rivers, due to gentle slope, the velocity of rivers decreases. This results in the formation of riverine islands. Majuli, in the Brahmaputra river, is the largest inhabited riverine island in the world. Rivers in their lower course split into numerous channels due to silt deposition. These channels are known as distributaries.

The Northern Plain is broadly divided into three sections. The Western part is referred to as the Punjab Plains, formed by the Indus and its tributaries. The larger part of this plain lies in Pakistan. Major tributaries include the Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas, and Satluj which originate in the Himalaya. This section is dominated by the doabs (areas between two rivers).

The Ganga plain extends between Ghaggar and Teesta rivers. It is spread over North India including Haryana, Delhi, U.P., Bihar, partly Jharkhand and West Bengal. In the East, particularly in Assam, lies the Brahmaputra plain.

The Northern plains are generally described as flat land, but actually have diverse relief features. According to variations in relief, the Northern plains can be divided into four regions: Bhabar, Terai, Bhangar, and Khadar.

topics.topic_name: Relief Features of the Northern Plains

content.text: The Bhabar is a narrow belt of about 8 to 16 km width lying parallel to the slopes of the Shiwaliks. Rivers deposit pebbles here. All streams disappear in this bhabar belt due to the porous nature of sediments.

South of the bhabar belt, the streams and rivers re-emerge and create a wet, swampy, and marshy region known as the Terai. This was a thickly forested region full of wildlife. The forests have been cleared to create agricultural land and to settle migrants from Pakistan after partition.

The largest part of the Northern plain is formed of older alluvium and lies above the floodplains of the rivers, presenting a terrace-like feature. This part is known as the Bhangar. The soil contains calcareous deposits, locally known as kankar.
The newer, younger deposits of the floodplains are called Khadar. They are renewed almost every year through periodic flooding, making them fertile and ideal for intensive agriculture.

The Peninsular plateau is a tableland composed of old crystalline, igneous, and metamorphic rocks. It was formed due to the breaking and drifting of the Gondwana land, making it part of the oldest landmass on Earth. The plateau has broad and shallow valleys and rounded hills.

The plateau consists of two broad divisions: the Central Highlands and the Deccan Plateau. The part north of the Narmada river, covering the major area of the Malwa plateau, is known as the Central Highlands. The part south of the Narmada is the Deccan Plateau.

The Central Highlands are bounded by the Vindhyan range, which is bounded by the Satpura range on the south and the Aravalis on the northwest. The further westward extension gradually merges with the sandy and rocky desert of Rajasthan. The rivers draining this region (Chambal, Sind, Betwa, Ken) flow from southwest to northeast, indicating the slope of the region.

The Central Highlands are wider in the west but narrower in the east. The eastward extensions are locally known as Bundelkhand and Baghelkhand. The Chotanagpur plateau marks the further eastward extension, drained by the Damodar river.

The Deccan Plateau is a triangular landmass that lies south of the Narmada river. The Satpura range flanks its broad base in the north, while the Mahadev, Kaimur hills, and Maikal range form its eastern extensions. The Deccan Plateau is higher in the west and slopes gently eastwards.

An extension of the Plateau is visible in the northeast, locally known as Meghalaya, Karbi-Anglong Plateau, and North Cachar Hills. It is separated by a fault from the Chotanagpur Plateau. Three prominent hill ranges from west to east are the Garo, Khasi, and Jaintia hills.

topics.topic_name: The Ghats: Eastern and Western Edges

content.text: The Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats mark the western and eastern edges of the Deccan Plateau respectively. The Western Ghats lie parallel to the western coast. They are continuous and can be crossed only through passes. The Thal, Bhor, and Pal Ghats are important passes.

The Western Ghats are higher than the Eastern Ghats. Their average elevation is 900–1,600 metres as against 600 metres of the Eastern Ghats. The Western Ghats cause orographic rain by facing the rain-bearing moist winds to rise along the western slopes. The highest peaks include Anai Mudi (2,695 metres) and Doda Betta (2,637 metres).

The Eastern Ghats stretch from the Mahanadi Valley to the Nigiris in the south. They are discontinuous and irregular, dissected by rivers draining into the Bay of Bengal. Mahendragiri (1,501 metres) is the highest peak in the Eastern Ghats. The Shevroy Hills and Javadi Hills are located to the southeast.

topics.topic_name: The Deccan Trap and Other Features

content.text: One of the distinct features of the Peninsular plateau is the black soil area known as the Deccan Trap. This volcanic deposit of igneous rocks has denuded over time and is responsible for the formation of black soil, which is highly fertile.

The Aravali Hills lie on the western and northwestern margins of the Peninsular plateau. These are highly eroded hills and are found as broken hills. They extend from Gujarat to Delhi in a southwest-northeast direction.

The Indian Desert lies towards the western margins of the Aravali Hills. It is an undulating sandy plain covered with sand dunes. This region receives very low rainfall, below 150 mm per year. It has an arid climate with low vegetation cover.
Streams appear during the rainy season. They soon disappear into the sand as they do not have enough water to reach the sea. The Luni is the only large river in this region.

Barchans (crescent-shaped dunes) cover larger areas of the desert. Longitudinal dunes become more prominent near the Indo-Pakistan boundary. The landscape is dominated by sand formations and sparse vegetation adapted to extreme aridity.

The Peninsular plateau is flanked by stretches of narrow coastal strips, running along the Arabian Sea on the west and the Bay of Bengal on the east. These coastal plains are important for fishing, ports, and trade.

The western coast, sandwiched between the Western Ghats and the Arabian Sea, is a narrow plain. It consists of three sections: the northern part called the Konkan (Mumbai – Goa), the central stretch called the Kannad Plain, and the southern stretch referred to as the Malabar coast.

The plains along the Bay of Bengal are wide and level. In the northern part, it is referred to as the Northern Circar, while the southern part is known as the Coromandel Coast. Large rivers such as the Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri have formed extensive deltas on this coast.
Lake Chilika is an important feature along the eastern coast. Located in Odisha, it is the largest salt water lake in India, lying south of the Mahanadi delta.

Besides the vast mainland, India has two groups of islands. The Lakshadweep Islands are located close to the Malabar coast of Kerala. This group of islands is composed of small coral islands. Originally known as Laccadive, Minicoy, and Amindive, they were renamed Lakshadweep in 1973.

Lakshadweep covers a small area of 32 sq km and includes 36 islands. Kavaratti island is the administrative headquarters. This island group has great diversity of flora and fauna. Pitti island, which is uninhabited, has a bird sanctuary.

The Andaman and Nicobar Islands form an elongated chain in the Bay of Bengal extending from north to south. They are larger in size and more numerous than Lakshadweep Islands. The entire group is divided into two broad categories: the Andaman in the north and the Nicobar in the south. These islands are believed to be an elevated portion of submarine mountains and are of great strategic importance for the country.

Both island groups have great diversity of flora and fauna. The Andaman and Nicobar Islands lie close to the equator and experience equatorial climate with thick forest cover. India's only active volcano is found on Barren Island in the Andaman and Nicobar group of islands.

topics.topic_name: How Diverse Physical Features Support Each Other

content.text: A detailed account of the different physiographic units shows the unique features of each region. However, it is clear that each region complements the other and makes the country richer in its natural resources. The mountains are the major sources of water and forest wealth. The northern plains are the granaries of the country, providing food for the population. The plateau is a storehouse of minerals, playing a crucial role in industrialisation. The coastal regions and island groups provide sites for fishing and port activities.

Thus, the diverse physical features of the land have immense future possibilities of development. India's varied geography creates a comprehensive resource base supporting different economic activities in different regions while maintaining national unity.

glossary.definition: Major geographical regions of India classified based on distinct physical features, geology, and topography.
Mountains formed by folding of the earth's crust due to tectonic forces; the Himalayas are young fold mountains.
Sediment deposits (sand, silt, clay) carried and deposited by rivers; forms the Northern Plains.
An elevated area of land with relatively flat surface and steep sides; the Peninsular Plateau is India's major plateau.
A deep, narrow valley with steep sides, often formed by river erosion through hard rock.
A large mass of ice in mountains that moves slowly downward; common in the Himalayas.
A longitudinal valley found between the Lesser Himalayas and the Shiwaliks; examples include Dehra Dun.
An area of land between two rivers; found in the Punjab Plains formed by Indus tributaries.
A narrow belt of porous sediments along the Shiwaliks where streams disappear underground.
A wet, swampy, and marshy region south of the Bhabar, originally forested and full of wildlife.
Older alluvium deposits in the Northern Plains above floodplains, containing kankar (calcareous deposits).
Newer, younger alluvium deposits in river floodplains, renewed annually and highly fertile for agriculture.
A mountain range or the edge of a plateau; the Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats border the Deccan Plateau.
Rainfall caused by moist winds being forced to rise over mountains, losing moisture as they ascend.
Volcanic deposits of igneous rocks covering parts of the Peninsular Plateau; source of black soil.
Highly fertile soil formed from weathered volcanic rocks; found in the Deccan region.
A crescent-shaped sand dune found in deserts, common in the Indian Desert.
Channels formed when a river splits into multiple branches due to sediment deposition; common in deltas.
An island formed in the middle of a river due to sediment deposition; Majuli is the world's largest.
A triangular or fan-shaped area where a river deposits sediment at its mouth; formed by rivers entering seas.

summary_points: India has six major physiographic divisions: Himalayan Mountains (2,400 km long, young fold mountains with 3 parallel ranges), Northern Plains (7 lakh sq km, formed by Indus-Ganga-Brahmaputra alluvial deposits), Peninsular Plateau (ancient tableland with Central Highlands and Deccan Plateau), Indian Desert (arid region <150mm rainfall), Coastal Plains (narrow strips on both coasts), and Islands (Lakshadweep and Andaman-Nicobar groups).

Himalayas consist of three ranges: Great Himalayas/Himadri (average 6,000m, loftiest peaks, snow-bound), Lesser Himalayas/Himachal (3,700-4,500m, famous valleys and hill stations), and Shiwaliks/Outer Himalayas (900-1,100m, composed of unconsolidated sediments); divided regionally into Punjab, Kumaon, Nepal, and Assam Himalayas plus Purvachal in northeast.

Northern Plains have four relief features: Bhabar (porous belt losing streams), Terai (swampy, originally forested), Bhangar (older alluvium above floodplains with kankar deposits), and Khadar (youngest alluvium renewed annually, highly fertile); divided into Punjab Plains (Indus tributaries, doabs), Ganga Plain, and Brahmaputra Plain.

Peninsular Plateau is ancient Gondwana landmass composed of metamorphic rocks with black soil (Deccan Trap), divided into Central Highlands (north of Narmada, Vindhyan-bounded) and Deccan Plateau (south of Narmada, triangular); Western Ghats (900-1,600m, continuous, orographic rainfall, higher) and Eastern Ghats (600m average, discontinuous) mark western and eastern edges.

Indian Desert lies west of Aravalis receiving <150mm rainfall, characterized by barchans and sand dunes, with Luni as only major river; arid climate with sparse vegetation adapted to extreme water scarcity.

Coastal Plains comprise narrow western coast between Ghats and Arabian Sea (Konkan, Kannad, Malabar sections) and wide eastern coast along Bay of Bengal (Northern Circar, Coromandel) with river deltas and Chilika Lake (largest saltwater lake).

Island groups include Lakshadweep (36 coral islands, 32 sq km near Malabar coast, administrative capital Kavaratti, bird sanctuary at Pitti) and Andaman-Nicobar (larger chain in Bay of Bengal, equatorial climate, thick forests, only active volcano at Barren Island).

Diverse physiographic regions complement each other: mountains provide water and forests, plains are agricultural granaries, plateau contains mineral wealth, coasts enable fishing and ports, creating comprehensive resource base supporting different activities while maintaining national unity.
